hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Shopping, Returning, and Negotiating Price -- Being Direct and Polite in American Stores
lesson.menu_desc: Da devolucao de um casaco defeituoso a negociar preco: used to para habitos que mudaram, e como ser direta e educada no balcao -- exchange, return policy, price match, store credit -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 9 -- Shopping, Returning, and Negotiating Price
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 9 -- Shopping, Returning, and Negotiating Price

listenings.text: Hello, this is a message for Lucimara Ito. My name is Rachel, and I'm calling from the returns team at Nordstrom about the jacket you sent back last week. I wanted to reach you directly, because I have good news and one small question. First, the good news. You told us the zipper was broken, and you were right. The item was defective, so this is not a normal return. That means the fifteen percent restocking fee does not apply to you. I have already removed it from your account. Now, about the refund. Because the jacket was defective, you have a choice that most customers do not get. You can have the full amount back on your original card, or you can take it as store credit, and if you choose store credit, we will add an extra ten percent as an apology. That is entirely up to you. Just so you know, our normal return policy is thirty days with the receipt, but for a defective item there is no time limit at all. One more thing. I saw that you are part of our loyalty program, so I have added the points for this purchase back to your account as well. If you would like the refund on your card, you do not need to do anything. If you would prefer the store credit, just reply to this message or call us back. Thank you, and I'm sorry for the trouble.

Welcome back to The Everyday Traveler. Today, one thing that surprises almost every visitor to the United States: how easy it is to return something. Here is what you need to understand. In a lot of the world, once you buy it, it is yours. In America, the return is part of the deal. Big stores compete on it. So the first rule is simple: keep your receipt, and check the return policy before you buy, not after. Most stores give you thirty days. Some give you a full year. Now, I used to think you had to accept whatever price was on the tag. You do not. Two words changed how I shop here: price match. If you find the same item cheaper somewhere else, show them, and many stores will match it on the spot. And here is the one that people from countries with strong consumer laws get wrong. If something is defective, you are not asking for a favor. You have a right. So do not apologize. Be polite, be calm, but be clear: the item is broken, and you would like a refund or an exchange. Keep the receipt, know the policy, and remember that in an American store, the customer really does have the upper hand. Use it kindly, and use it well.

items.situation: The jacket you bought yesterday has a broken zipper. You are at the counter with the receipt. Ask to return it.
tips: I'd like to return this jacket -- the zipper is broken, so it's defective. Here's the receipt.
Because it's defective, you are not asking for a favor. Be polite, but state the fact clearly.

items.situation: You want the same shirt in a bigger size. You do not want your money back -- you want a swap.
tips: Could I exchange this for a large? The medium doesn't fit quite right.
Exchange = same item, different size or color. Not a refund.

items.situation: The cashier says the item is 'final sale' and cannot be returned, but it is defective. Advocate for yourself.
tips: I understand it's final sale, but the item is defective -- does that change the return policy?
Final sale usually blocks returns of choice, not of defective goods. Ask, calmly.

items.situation: You found the same speaker fifty dollars cheaper on another store's website. Ask this store to match it.
tips: Do you do a price match? I found this exact model for fifty dollars less online.
Have the proof ready on your phone. Ask directly -- many stores say yes.

items.situation: They can only give you store credit, not cash, because you lost the receipt. Decide and respond politely.
tips: That works -- I'll take the store credit. Could you tell me how long it's valid for?
Store credit = money only for that store. Fair when you have no receipt.

items.situation: At a small market in Lyon, a scarf is priced high. You want a better deal. Open the negotiation.
tips: It's beautiful -- is this your best price? I used to pay less for one like this at home.
'Is this your best price?' is the polite door into a negotiation. Stay friendly.
